goal: Stabilize cocoa butter crystals to produce a glossy, snappy chocolate with a smooth texture.
when_to_use: When melting chocolate for coating, molding, or dipping where a professional, stable finish is required.
steps: Finely chop the chocolate for even melting.
Heat the chocolate to a specific melting temperature to melt all crystals: 45–50°C (113–122°F) for dark chocolate, 40–45°C (104–113°F) for milk and white chocolate.
Cool the chocolate to a specific cooling temperature to form stable crystals: 28–29°C (82–84°F) for dark, 27–28°C (80–82°F) for milk, 24–25°C (74–76°F) for white.

While cooling, stir or work the chocolate on a cool surface to encourage the formation of stable crystals. This can be done by seeding with solid tempered chocolate or by stirring/working until it thickens.

Gently reheat the chocolate to the final tempering range to melt any unstable crystals: 31–32°C (88–90°F) for dark, 30–31°C (86–88°F) for milk, 27–28°C (80–82°F) for white. Stir well to ensure an even temperature.
critical_control_points: Do not exceed the melting temperature, or all crystals will be destroyed.
Maintain the chocolate within the tempering range after it has been tempered.
If using the seed method, ensure the seed chocolate is itself in temper.
Avoid introducing any moisture, which will cause the chocolate to seize.

failure_modes.result: Mottled, grey appearance; soft texture; lacks snap; difficult to unmold.
Loss of all stable crystals; chocolate will be out of temper.
Chocolate seizes into a stiff, grainy paste.

signals.visual: When testing on a cool surface, properly tempered chocolate sets in a few minutes with a clean, silky, shiny surface. The side in contact with the surface is glossy.
signals.touch: Hardens with a firm snap at room temperature, not soft or greasy.

output.appearance: glossy, uniform, with a clean shine

goal: Create a smooth, stable mixture of chocolate and cream that can be used as a filling, glaze, or sauce.
when_to_use: When preparing fillings for truffles, cakes, or pastries, or as a sauce for desserts.
steps: Finely chop the chocolate and place it in a heatproof bowl.
In a saucepan, bring the cream just to a simmer (scalding).
Pour the hot cream over the chopped chocolate.
Let the mixture stand for 30–60 seconds to allow the chocolate to melt.
Gently stir from the center outwards with a whisk or spatula until the mixture is smooth and emulsified.
If using butter or other flavorings, incorporate them at this stage while the ganache is still warm.
critical_control_points: Do not let the cream boil vigorously, as this can cause the emulsion to break.
If the chocolate is not fully melted by the residual heat, warm the mixture briefly in a double boiler, stirring constantly.
Do not stir aggressively, especially in the initial stages, to avoid incorporating air and creating bubbles.
Do not add cold liquid to the warm ganache, as this can cause the emulsion to separate.

failure_modes.case: Ganache is overheated or cooled too quickly
failure_modes.result: Oil separates and the mixture becomes greasy or grainy.

failure_modes.case: Too much liquid added to warm chocolate
failure_modes.result: The emulsion can break, leading to separation.
Can cause the chocolate to overheat and the fat to separate.

signals.visual: Smooth, homogenous mixture that is thick and glossy.
signals.touch: Warm and fluid, thickens as it cools.

output.texture: smooth, thick, and creamy (soft or firm depending on chocolate-to-cream ratio)

goal: Create a browned crust via Maillard reaction on meats, fish, or vegetables.
when_to_use: When cooking protein or vegetables where a flavorful, crispy surface is desired and a quick cooking time is appropriate.
steps: Preheat pan until very hot (surface temperature approx. 200°C).
Add a small amount of high smoke point oil to the pan.
Place food in the pan without moving it.
Allow crust to form undisturbed for 60–120 seconds, depending on thickness.
Flip the food once and repeat the process on the other side.
Optional: Baste with butter and aromatics in the last minute of cooking.
critical_control_points: Pan must be fully preheated before adding food.
Surface of the food must be dry; pat with a paper towel if necessary.
Do not overcrowd the pan; leave space between pieces to avoid steaming.
Do not move the food during the initial sear; it will release naturally when the crust is formed.

failure_modes.result: Meat will steam, resulting in a grey color and no crust.
Moisture will cool the pan and cause steaming instead of searing.
Temperature drops, causing steaming and uneven browning.
Crust will tear and food will stick to the pan.

signals.visual: Deep golden to dark brown crust forms on the contact surface. Maillard browning is visible.
signals.sound: Loud, continuous sizzling sound. If the sizzle weakens, the pan temperature has dropped too much.
signals.touch: Food releases easily from the pan when the crust is properly formed.

goal: Partially cook vegetables or fruit to inactivate enzymes, set color, or loosen skins.
when_to_use: Before freezing vegetables, before peeling tomatoes or stone fruits, or to par-cook vegetables for stir-fries or salads.
steps: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil.
Add salt to the water if desired (approx. 2 tablespoons per quart).
Prepare an ice water bath in a large bowl.
Add the vegetables or fruit to the boiling water.
Cook for a short, specified time (e.g., 1–3 minutes for most vegetables, 15–60 seconds for peeling fruits).
Immediately remove the food from the boiling water and plunge it into the ice water bath to stop the cooking process.
Once cooled, drain and dry the blanched food.
critical_control_points: Use a large volume of water to maintain a boil when food is added.
Cook time is critical and depends on the size and type of food.
Shocking in ice water is essential to stop the cooking immediately and preserve color, texture, and flavor.
If blanching for freezing, ensure the food is thoroughly dried before packaging to prevent ice crystal formation.

failure_modes.result: Overcooked, mushy vegetables; loss of nutrients and flavor; skins of fruits may break down.
Temperature drops when food is added, leading to uneven cooking.
Vegetables continue to cook from residual heat, resulting in loss of bright color and crisp texture.

signals.visual: Color becomes brighter and more vivid (for green vegetables). The food may become slightly translucent. Skins of fruits will start to split or wrinkle.
signals.sound: Rapid bubbling resumes shortly after food is added, indicating the water has returned to a boil.
signals.touch: Vegetables become slightly tender but still firm.

output.flavor: fresh, slightly cooked, with enhanced sweetness in some vegetables

goal: Create a stable emulsion of oil in water (or acid) using egg yolk as an emulsifier.
when_to_use: When making a stable, thick, creamy sauce or dressing from oil, acid, and egg yolks.
equipment: dropper or small pitcher for oil (optional)
steps: Ensure all ingredients are at room temperature.
In a bowl, combine the egg yolk(s), a small amount of the acid, salt, and mustard if using.
Whisk the mixture until it is well combined and slightly thickened.
Start adding the oil very slowly, drop by drop, while whisking constantly.
As the mixture thickens and the emulsion forms, the oil can be added in a thin, steady stream while continuing to whisk.
Once a thick, stable emulsion is formed, whisk in the remaining acid and any other flavorings. Adjust seasoning.
critical_control_points: All ingredients should be at room temperature.
Start with a small amount of the water phase (acid and yolk) and add oil very slowly at the beginning.
Add oil gradually; adding too much oil too quickly will cause the emulsion to break.
If using a whisk, whisk constantly to ensure the oil is sheared into small droplets.

failure_modes.result: Mayonnaise will break (separate into liquid and oily phases).
Emulsion may not form properly or will be less stable.

failure_modes.case: Too much water phase relative to oil
failure_modes.result: Mayonnaise will be thin and runny.
Mayonnaise can become too thick, or the emulsion can break from shear.

signals.visual: Mixture becomes thick, creamy, and pale yellow. When stable, it will hold its shape.
signals.sound: Whisking sound becomes muffled as mixture thickens.
signals.touch: Thick and viscous, coats the back of a spoon without running off.

output.appearance: opaque and pale yellow to white

goal: Create a laminated dough with many alternating layers of dough and butter that will expand dramatically when baked.
when_to_use: When making a delicate, flaky, and very light pastry for items like palmiers, vol-au-vents, or mille-feuille.
inputs: butter (for the dough and for the butter block)
steps: Prepare a dough (détrempe) by mixing flour, water, salt, and a small amount of butter. Mix minimally, form into a square, and chill.
Prepare a butter block by shaping cold butter into a square slab about half the size of the dough square.
Place the butter block on the chilled dough square at a 45-degree angle, then fold the dough flaps over the butter to enclose it completely.
Roll the dough-butter package into a long rectangle, about 3 times as long as it is wide.
Fold the rectangle into thirds, like a business letter (this is one 'turn'). Wrap and chill for 30 minutes.
Repeat the rolling and folding process, giving the dough 5-6 total turns, chilling for 30 minutes between each turn.
After the final turn, chill the dough for at least an hour before shaping and baking.
critical_control_points: Keep the dough and butter at a cool temperature throughout the process; if they get too warm, the butter will melt into the dough, ruining the layers.
The consistency of the dough and butter block must be similar; if the butter is too hard, it will shatter, and if too soft, it will squeeze out.
Roll evenly to ensure uniform layers.
Chill the dough between turns to relax the gluten and re-chill the butter.
Do not let the dough rest too long between turns, as this can allow the gluten to set and make rolling difficult.

failure_modes.case: Dough or butter gets too warm
failure_modes.result: Butter melts into the dough, layers are lost, and the pastry will not puff.
Butter will break through the dough layers during rolling, creating an uneven texture.
Insufficient layering, leading to poor rise and flakiness.
Dough may tear, exposing the butter and ruining the lamination.

signals.visual: Dough should have distinct, even layers visible at the cut edge. It should be smooth and not greasy.
signals.touch: Dough should feel cool and firm to the touch, but pliable enough to roll.

output.texture: extremely flaky, light, and crisp when baked
output.appearance: many distinct layers that puff up dramatically in the oven

goal: Concentrate flavor and create a tender, caramelized exterior through dry heat.
when_to_use: For a wide variety of root vegetables and hardy squashes to bring out their natural sweetness and add depth of flavor.
steps: Preheat oven to between 375°F and 425°F (190–220°C).
Cut vegetables into uniform-sized pieces to ensure even cooking.
Toss the vegetables with oil, salt, and any herbs or spices in a mixing bowl until evenly coated.
Spread vegetables in a single layer on a baking sheet, leaving space between pieces to allow for browning and to prevent steaming.
Roast in the preheated oven, flipping or stirring halfway through, until tender and caramelized.
critical_control_points: Cut vegetables into uniform sizes to ensure even cooking.
Do not overcrowd the pan; this will cause them to steam instead of roast.
A high enough oven temperature is needed to achieve browning before the vegetables dry out.
Flip or stir once to promote even browning on all sides.

failure_modes.result: Vegetables steam, becoming soft and pale instead of brown and crisp.
Vegetables dry out before browning or become mushy.
Some pieces overcook while others remain undercooked.

signals.visual: Vegetables have golden-brown, caramelized edges and are tender when pierced with a fork.
signals.sound: Gentle sizzling sound from the pan when the door is opened.
signals.touch: Vegetables are easily pierced with a fork or paring knife.

output.texture: tender interior with a crisp, caramelized exterior
output.appearance: golden to dark brown edges, deeper color than raw
